tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task with unknown disease, the agent should identify dehydration as the primary working diagnosis and document it clearly to guide immediate supportive care (e.g., IV fluids) and monitoring.

nl_assertions: Elicits key dehydration features (poor intake, vomiting/diarrhea, decreased urine output, dizziness/orthostasis, thirst) and assesses severity (mental status, ability to tolerate PO, orthostatic symptoms).
Asks about red flags requiring urgent escalation (syncope, confusion, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool/emesis, persistent high fever, chest pain, severe shortness of breath).
Assesses hydration status with focused questions and/or exam language (dry mucous membranes, tachycardia, orthostatic hypotension, capillary refill, skin turgor).

Explains the working diagnosis using the required terms, explicitly including both 'dehydration' and 'IV fluids', and provides a brief rationale for why IV fluids may be needed (e.g., inability to keep fluids down or signs of moderate/severe dehydration).
Provides a clear safety-net plan (when to seek urgent care/ED) and follow-up expectations.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent does prescribe, it should have first addressed required safety checks conceptually (contraindications before prescribing; allergies ideally checked) and avoid unsafe choices.

Avoids overconfident etiologic labeling when data are limited; acknowledges uncertainty and offers reasonable differentials (e.g., gastroenteritis, heat illness, medication-related diuresis) without derailing from the primary diagnosis of dehydration.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly records the primary diagnosis for an L1 benchmark; establishes the basis for counseling, genetic confirmation, and supportive management planning.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "atypical Rett syndrome" and avoids labeling as classic Rett unless criteria are met.

Explains that Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental disorder often due to a genetic variant, and explicitly mentions "genetic testing" as part of confirmation/etiologic evaluation (e.g., MECP2 and/or broader neurodevelopmental gene panel depending on presentation).
Explains treatment as primarily "supportive care" (multidisciplinary therapies, symptom management) and does not imply a curative medication.
Uses clear, family-centered language appropriate for caregivers; checks understanding and invites questions.

Does not prescribe medications in an L1 diagnosis-only task; if discussing potential future meds (e.g., for seizures/spasticity), frames them as conditional and individualized rather than initiated now.

If any medication is proposed hypothetically or for comorbidities, performs the required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check (both critical) and documents/acknowledges them in reasoning.

Addresses key safety/anticipatory guidance topics relevant to Rett spectrum (e.g., seizures, breathing irregularities, feeding/swallowing concerns) and advises urgent evaluation for red flags without over-triaging routine issues.
Avoids misinformation (e.g., vaccines causing Rett, guaranteed prognosis); communicates uncertainty and variability in atypical presentations.

minimal_paths.description: Identify atypical Rett syndrome as the most likely diagnosis, recommend genetic testing for confirmation, and outline supportive care and follow-up without prescribing.
must_collect: loss of purposeful hand skills or reduced hand use
hand stereotypies (wringing, washing, clapping) or other repetitive hand movements
head growth trajectory (deceleration may be present but can be absent in atypical cases)

steps: Elicit history focusing on neurodevelopmental course, regression/plateau, stereotypies, mobility, seizures, breathing, feeding, and growth/head circumference trajectory.
Perform/describe focused neuro exam and review prior records (developmental assessments, EEG if seizures, growth charts).
Explain the working diagnosis as atypical Rett syndrome and why (pattern of regression/plateau with characteristic features, acknowledging atypical variability).
Recommend genetic testing explicitly (MECP2 sequencing/deletion-duplication; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 or broader panel based on phenotype) and discuss implications for family counseling.
Explain treatment as supportive care: PT/OT/speech, feeding evaluation, seizure surveillance/management planning, scoliosis monitoring, sleep and behavioral supports.
Provide safety net advice (seek urgent care for prolonged seizures, aspiration/choking, respiratory distress, dehydration, significant feeding decline).
Arrange follow-up plan (timeline for results review and multidisciplinary coordination) without prescribing medications.

steps: Gather key regression and stereotypy history plus seizure/breathing/feeding review.
Discuss differential considerations (e.g., autism with regression, cerebral palsy, metabolic/mitochondrial disorders, other genetic encephalopathies) and why atypical Rett is favored.
State atypical Rett syndrome as the working diagnosis and recommend genetic testing.
Outline supportive care and multidisciplinary supports; provide red-flag guidance and follow-up.

Screens for red flags requiring emergency escalation: new focal neurologic deficits, seizure, altered mental status, persistent worst headache, neck stiffness/fever, severe hypertension, pregnancy/postpartum, anticoagulant use, immunosuppression.

Acknowledges important differentials for thunderclap headache and indicates need to exclude them (e.g., aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage, cervical artery dissection, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, pituitary apoplexy, meningitis/encephalitis, intracerebral hemorrhage).
Avoids recommending glucocorticoids as routine therapy for RCVS (may worsen outcomes) and avoids suggesting triptans/ergots as headache treatment in suspected RCVS.
If discussing treatment at a high level, includes communication milestone elements: mentions a 'calcium channel blocker' as commonly used and advises to 'avoid triggers' and vasoactive substances.

tool_call_sequence.description: Correctly identifies cluster headache to enable appropriate acute and preventive management and avoid mismanagement of secondary headache causes.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as cluster headache (familial context may be noted but does not replace diagnostic criteria).
Elicits key cluster headache features: severe unilateral orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration (15–180 minutes), attack frequency up to multiple/day, and circadian/cluster periodicity.

Asks about ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, nasal congestion/rhinorrhea, eyelid edema, facial sweating, miosis/ptosis) and/or restlessness/agitation during attacks.

Screens for red flags/secondary headache features (sudden thunderclap onset, new neurologic deficit, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression/cancer, age >50 new onset, progressive change, head trauma) and escalates appropriately if present.
Documents relevant family history of similar attacks and distinguishes from migraine (photophobia/phonophobia may occur but restlessness and autonomic signs support cluster).
Communication milestone: explains the diagnosis using the term 'cluster headache' and briefly describes why the presentation fits.
Communication milestone: explains evidence-based acute treatments including high-flow oxygen and a triptan as first-line options.

Performs required safety checks before any prescribing is suggested/initiated: confirms allergies (non-critical), and explicitly checks for contraindications and drug interactions for triptans (critical) such as ischemic heart disease, uncontrolled hypertension, stroke/TIA, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, severe hepatic impairment, recent ergot use, or MAOI use.
Avoids unsafe recommendations: does not recommend opioids for cluster headache; does not recommend ergot/triptan co-administration within recommended washout windows.

Provides a follow-up/when-to-seek-care plan in natural language (even though no follow-up tool is required at L1), including urgent evaluation for new neurologic deficits, atypical features, or thunderclap headache.

Screen for secondary headache red flags and atypical features; advise urgent evaluation if present.
Explain diagnosis explicitly as cluster headache and how features fit.
Explain first-line acute options: high-flow oxygen and a triptan; mention that preventive therapy may be needed during a cluster period.
Record diagnosis with record_diagnosis.

steps: Elicit core cluster features plus migraine/TAC distinguishing points (restlessness, autonomic signs, short duration).

Complete red-flag screen and advise escalation if present.
